coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional halogen doped carbon quantum dots for photo-degradation of methylene blue and selective detection of Cu2+ ions

coredata.dc:description: Urbanization and rising demand lead to hazardous industrial residue, particularly organic dyes and chemicals, polluting water bodies. This study explores halogen-doped carbon dots for photocatalytic degradation of carcinogenic methylene blue. Electron-withdrawing halogen ions were found to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. The paper systematically examines the influence of chlorine, fluorine, iodine, and bromine doping on methylene blue photodegradation. Halogen doping increased sample crystallinity and reduced particle size, causing a blue shift in absorption spectra. A wider band gap potentially reduced charge carrier recombination, improving separation. Iodine-doped carbon dots exhibited the highest methylene blue adsorption. Bromine doping boosted the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of undoped carbon dots from 90 % to 98.7 % under 60 min of UV irradiation. LC-MS studies elucidated the degradation mechanism. Detailed optical, luminescent, compositional, and morphological analyses were conducted. Intrinsic carbon dots were also utilized as fluorescent probes for selective sensing of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution. Carbon dots were found to selectively detect the presence of Cu2+ ions out of Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, Co2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions. Cu2+ ions get adsorbed on the oxygen-functional groups on the carbon dot surface and form a ground state complex, resulting in static quenching of blue luminescence of carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction pattern of as-synthesized carbon dots.

(a, b) High resolution transmission electron microscopy images of undoped carbon dots at different magnification and (c, d), high resolution transmission electron microscopy images of bromine doped carbon dots (insets show the SAED pattern of undoped and doped carbon dots respectively.)
Raman spectra of undoped and halogen-doped carbon dots.
FT-IR Spectroscopy of halogen doped and undoped carbon dots.

XPS survey plot of (a) intrinsic carbon dots (UCD) (b) bromine–doped carbon dots and (c-d) high-resolution scan of elements namely C, O in intrinsic carbon quantum dots, (e-g) high-resolution scan of elements C, O, and Br in Br-doped carbon quantum dots.
UV–visible spectroscopy of un-doped and halogen-doped carbon dots.
Zeta Potential of un-doped and halogen doped carbon dots.

Photocatalytic degradation through UV–visible spectra of (a) un-doped carbon dots, (b) Fluorine-doped CDs, (c) Chlorine-doped CDs (d) Bromine-doped CDs, (e) Iodine-doped CDs and (f) reduction in MB dye concentration (C/Co) over time.

Schematic representation for mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of MB molecules – (a) adsorption of MB on surface functional groups on carbon dot surface, (b) change in MB molecular structure, (c) generation of electron-hole pairs in carbon dots under UV irradiation, (d) attack of reactive ion species on MB molecules, (e) MB molecules converts into CO2, SO2, NOx, H2O.
Mechanistic pathways of degradation of Methylene Blue (MB).

(a) Selectivity test of carbon dots for metal ions, (b) fluorescence quenching mechanism by metal ion‑carbon dot complex formation, (c) fluorescent intensities of carbon quantum dots when exposed to different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0–1000 μM) (d) Stern-Volmer plot indicating relative intensity vs. concentration of Cu2+ ions).
CQD samples used as a photocatalyst for dye degradation in literature.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional halogen doped carbon quantum dots for photo-degradation of methylene blue and selective detection of Cu2+ ions

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Urbanization and rising demand lead to hazardous industrial residue, particularly organic dyes and chemicals, polluting water bodies. This study explores halogen-doped carbon dots for photocatalytic degradation of carcinogenic methylene blue. Electron-withdrawing halogen ions were found to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. The paper systematically examines the influence of chlorine, fluorine, iodine, and bromine doping on methylene blue photodegradation. Halogen doping increased sample crystallinity and reduced particle size, causing a blue shift in absorption spectra. A wider band gap potentially reduced charge carrier recombination, improving separation. Iodine-doped carbon dots exhibited the highest methylene blue adsorption. Bromine doping boosted the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of undoped carbon dots from 90 % to 98.7 % under 60 min of UV irradiation. LC-MS studies elucidated the degradation mechanism. Detailed optical, luminescent, compositional, and morphological analyses were conducted. Intrinsic carbon dots were also utilized as fluorescent probes for selective sensing of Cu2+ ions in aqueous solution. Carbon dots were found to selectively detect the presence of Cu2+ ions out of Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, Co2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions. Cu2+ ions get adsorbed on the oxygen-functional groups on the carbon dot surface and form a ground state complex, resulting in static quenching of blue luminescence of carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All the available data is presented in the manuscript and supplementary file.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is one of the most fundamental resources for life on Earth. It becomes more critical when only 0.01 % of the total volume of potable water is good enough for consumption [1,2]. Due to rapid industrialization and the lack of proper effluent management systems, water pollution has become a significant threat to human life and various ecosystems. The pollutants in the water include organic dyes, pesticides, pharmaceutical metabolites, heavy metal ions, micro- and nano-plastics, etc. [3–6]. Out of all the pollutants, textile dyes have contributed up to one-third of the pollution to potable water. Apart from textiles, dyes are also used in paper mills, the leather industry, cosmetics, inks, packaging industries, etc. The dyes used in such coating processes are formulated to resist any change in structure due to heat and light [7]. The conjugated electrons in the aromatic molecular structure make it even more resistant to any chemicals and provide high fastening effects. Methylene blue (MB) is one such cationic blue dye used as a stain for biomedical and therapeutic purposes [8]. It also has applications in printing, fashion industry, food industries, dye-sensitized solar cells, and other energy conversion technologies [8–10]. Dye molecules block sunlight for the ecosystem to flourish, changing the behavioural patterns of micro- and microorganisms like plankton, fishes, etc. These molecules also have carcinogenic and mutagenic properties [11,12]. The threat of bioaccumulation and bio-magnification along the food chain is also an important field of research at the moment. Carbon nanomaterials have been engineered to remove organic pollutants with the sustainable use of bio-waste. Carbon materials such as activated carbon, (2D) graphene, graphene-based material, (0D) carbon quantum dots, and (2D) graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) are being used to remove dye molecules by adsorption or as a catalyst to degrade them into harmless products like carbon dioxide, water, nitrous oxide, etc. [13–16]. Out of these, the carbon quantum dots are a potential candidate due to their small size, which gives them a higher surface-to-volume ratio for catalytic reactions on their surface. In addition to that, carbon dots have many applications in water remediation involving sensing, adsorption, and imaging [17–20]. However, most of the synthesized carbon dots have a low photodegradation efficiency, smaller charge carrier lifetime, and lower generation of oxidative radical species in the photoreaction, which limit the degradation efficiency [21–23]. Doped carbon dots and composites of carbon dots with semiconductors can improve the efficiency of the catalysis process [24–26]. Halogen doping can enhance the charge separation efficiency and induce an internal electric field, which may further increase charge separation efficiency. The electron-withdrawing nature of halogen species due to high electronegativity can further enhance the efficiency of the photocatalysis process. Further, heavy metal leaching in water sources is another prominent issue, not just organic dyes. Heavy metals such as Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Cr3+, and Pb2+ pose a severe threat to water bodies. Overexposure to all these heavy metal ions can lead to serious health conditions such as kidney failure, diabetes, liver damage, etc. In addition to photocatalysis, undoped carbon dots were found to be efficient fluorescent probes to sense the presence of Cu2+ ions in the aqueous solution.

A scanty amount of work has been reported on systematic studies on the role of halogen doping in photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Thus, to investigate the role of halogen doping, a series of samples of Cl, Br, I, and F-doped carbon dots samples were prepared, and their efficiency towards photocatalytic degradation of MB dye was studied and compared with that of the undoped carbon dots. Carbon dots were derived from ascorbic acid (Vitamin C), which provides a fast degradation of methylene blue (MB) without the assistance of H2O2 or a semiconductor. In this study, we observed that Br-doped carbon dots exhibited the highest efficiency compared to other halogen-doped and intrinsic carbon dots, resulting in 99.24 % degradation within 90 min under UV (nm) exposure. Mechanistic pathway for degradation of MB molecules into CO2 and H2O has been proposed. Undoped carbon dots were selectively sensitive towards Cu2+ ions compared to Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, and Co2+ ions. Cu2+ ions form complexes with the oxygen-functionalities over the surface of carbon dots, resulting in static quenching of luminescence from carbon dots.

Both undoped (UCDs) and halogen-doped carbon dots (*-XCDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal process. 0.5 g of ascorbic acid was used a precursor for the synthesis reaction for doped (XCDs) and undoped carbon dots (UCDs). A certain amount of DI water was added to ascorbic acid and ultra-sonicated until complete dissolution. For halogen doping, equal moles of halogen precursors (such as HF, KCl, KBr, KI) as that of the ascorbic acid were added to the ascorbic acid solution and sonicated for 15 mins. The obtained solution was transferred into a 100 ml stainless-steel Teflon-lined autoclave and filled using DI water. The autoclave was held at 180 °C for 8 h and was allowed to cool. As prepared yellow-brown solution was purified by ultrafiltration using a 0.22 μm porous cellulose nitrate membrane three times and the obtained supernatant was used for photodegradation studies. Analytical grade metals salts were procured from Sigma Aldrich namely – Cu(CH3COO)2 (Cu2+), FeCl3 (Fe3+), FeSO4 (Fe2+), Cr(NO3)2 (Cr2+), SnCl4 (Sn4+), Co(NO3)2 (Co2+) for conducting selectivity test of carbon dots towards sensing metal ions. Solutions with a concentration of 500 μM were made in 10 ml DI water to conduct the tests.

The obtained carbon dots were characterized structurally and optically to observe the underlying properties resulting in high degradation efficiencies. Structural characterization involved Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using the FEI-TECNAI G2-20 TWIN microscope to understand the size distribution of the carbon dots. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) Spectroscopy (Shimadzu, IR-Affinity-1) and Raman Spectroscopy (Horiba, XploRA Plus) were used to understand the elemental composition and give information about the carbon core. Optical properties were characterized using Agilent UV–Vis-NIR Spectrophotometer and Edinburgh FLS 1000 for UV absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy, respectively using a 10 mm quartz cuvette. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue was performed with a Heber multilamp photoreactor. Based on the optical absorption spectra of the carbon dots, 254 nm was found to be suitable to conduct the photocatalytic reaction. 1 l of MB stock solution was prepared (concentration = 10 ppm). 50 ml of the MB solution was used for the photocatalytic reaction. 10 ml of filtered carbon dots solution (undoped or halogen-doped carbon dots) was stirred with MB solution for 30 min for good adsorption, which may lead to a slight change of colour of the overall solution. The resulting solution was transferred to the test tube in the photoreactor, and the lamps were turned on. A small amount of sample was taken at specific intervals (10 min) to check the concentration of the methylene blue in the solution with the help of UV–visible spectroscopy. The resultant decrease in absorption intensities helped to gauge the degradation of MB molecules. For metal ion sensing, the carbon dots were tested for selectivity on various water-soluble metal ions (Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+). To identify the catalytic intermediates, LCMS was performed on the degraded and un-degraded samples with the help of XEVO-G2-XS-QTOF Mass Spectrometer. A 10 ml solution containing metal salts (500 μM) and 2 ml of the as-prepared carbon dots solution was used for the fluorescence spectroscopy. The solution was sonicated for 15 min before conducting the tests. The reduction of fluorescence emission intensities at 415 nm indicated the quenching of carbon dots emission by the metal ions.

Fig. 1 represents the X-ray diffraction pattern of the as-synthesized carbon dots. The slide was coated by drop casting the supernatant and heated at a temperature of 80 °C for 2 h to evaporate the solvent. The carbon dots exhibit a broad peak at 22.640 and 40.830 attributed to (002) and (100) respectively [27,28]. These planes indicate the formation of graphitic carbon with very poor crystallinity caused by the formation of additional oxygen-containing functional groups. The amorphous halo in the SAED pattern (Fig. 2 (a) inset) also confirms the formation of amorphous carbon dots, as the amorphous carbon structure is not able to diffract electrons appropriately [29].

To identify the shape and size distribution of the carbon dots, high-resolution transmission electron microscopic studies were carried out. In Fig. 2(a-d), the HR-TEM image exhibits monodispersed carbon dots with a quasi-spherical shape for both undoped and bromine-doped carbon dots. Spherical particles of very small size ∼9.65 nm can be seen from 2(a). Upon further magnification, individual particle can be seen along with the Moiré fringes. Diffused rings can be seen from SAED pattern (inset in Fig. 2(b)), which indicates that the carbon dot possess disordered or partially amorphous structure. No sharp diffraction spots can be seen from SAED pattern of the undoped (intrinsic) carbon dots. Lattice fringes arise due to the constructive interference of electrons which are diffracted by the regularly spaced atomic planes. Lattice fringes can be seen which indicates that there might be crystalline clusters that possess short range order. Poor crystalline nature of carbon dots can also be accessed by observing a broad diffraction peak in powder XRD studies. Lattice spacing of 0.2 nm corresponds to (1 0 0) plane of graphitic carbon as seen from Fig. 2(b) [30,31]. Br doped sample was found to exhibit uniform zero-dimensional particles with a smaller particle size of ∼4.4 nm in comparison to intrinsic sample as shown in Fig. 2(c). SAED pattern represent sharp diffraction spots which indicates the polycrystalline nature of Br doped carbon dot sample as seen in inset of Fig. 2(d).

Raman spectroscopy is an excellent spectroscopic tool as it characterizes the vibrational properties of inter‑carbon bonds (CC) and helps further understand the properties of carbon dots [32,33]. As seen from Fig. 3 , two prominent peaks can be observed in the Raman spectra. The observable peaks at ∼1350 cm−1 and ∼1580 cm−1 are attributed to the D band (defect band) and the G band (graphitic band), respectively. The occurrence of the D band in the Raman spectra of the carbon nanomaterials suggests the existence of defects in the graphitic structure and is attributed to the out-of-plane vibrations whereas the G band suggests the degree of sp2 hybridization of the aromatic ring due to the in-plane vibrations of ‘π’ orbitals of the carbon atoms [34]. Raman shift towards lower energy from the standard graphitic sample is observed in all the samples of the carbon dots, which implies the formation of amorphous carbon in the system [35]. The undoped (UCD) and doped carbon dots shows high-intensity peak near 1570 cm−1, which is attributed to the graphitic band and broader low-intensity peaks at 1340 cm−1 responsible for the defect band. Doping of halogen atoms has increased the intensity of the D- band as compared to undoped carbon dots which indicates clustering of the amorphous carbon [36]. The shift of D-band to lower energy implies the formation of distortions in six-fold or many-fold rings in the carbon clusters [32]. The same is also observed in the HR-TEM images in the form of the absence of graphitic fringes (Fig. 2(a)). It can be observed that there is an increase in the width of the G-band which indicates a higher bond angle between carbon atoms in the sp2 hybridized rings [37]. It can be inferred that the doping of halogen atoms has increased the distortions in the clusters to some extent. However, for bromine-doped carbon dots, the intensity and width of the D-band is seen to be reduced significantly, which represent that structures are less disordered as compared to the undoped carbon dot in favour of the clustering of carbon atoms [38]. The absence of the D-band suggests that the iodine doping may have led to a more ordered and graphitic-like structure compared to undoped carbon dots, thus D band intensity is very poor for iodine doped samples. In addition to G band, additional anomalous peak is observed in iodine doped carbon dots samples at 1076.3 cm-1. As per the literature, Raman peaks in the range of 990–1100 cm−1 arises due to the presence of aromatic rings [39]. Owing to the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon carbon skeleton might have resulted into a peak at 1076.3 cm−1.

Fourier Transform Infrared spectroscopic analysis of all the samples was carried out on the intrinsic and halogen-doped carbon dot samples to analyze the functional groups present on the surface of the carbon dots. Fig. 4 represents the FTIR spectroscopic vibrations related to functional groups on the carbon dot surface. Three prominent peaks are present in the FTIR spectra of undoped and halogen-doped samples. The broad absorption peak at 3283 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the hydroxyl group (-OH) [40,41]. The peak broadening suggests a strong interaction among the OH bonds of the carbon dots resulting in hydrogen bonding [42]. This enables an electrostatic interaction the positive moieties of the dye molecules as well. In FTIR spectra carbon dots, the absorption of IR spectra at a range of 1630 cm−1- 1680 cm−1 has been associated with a stretching vibration of the double bonds of carbon in the form of CC and CO. The aforementioned vibration has been reported to be attributed to carboxylic and ketonic groups for graphite-like carbon nanomaterials [43–45]. This implies the presence of graphitic rings on the carbon dot surface [46,47]. The aforementioned absorption confirms the presence of the oxygen-containing functional groups necessary for cationic dyes. The absorption peak at 630 cm−1 is attributed to the bending of -C–H bonds in the graphitic plane [48]. Doping of halogen ions doesn't result into any additional peaks in FTIR spectra.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy is a surface-sensitive measurement used to identify the functional groups, if any, present on the surface of intrinsic and bromine-doped carbon dots. XPS study was restricted to undoped and optimised sample of bromine-doped carbon dots only. The carbon quantum dots were drop cast on glass slides for the same. The elemental survey scan was characterized using a K-ALPHA X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an electron gun of power 0.5 eV. XPS spectra of intrinsic carbon dots were compared with that of the bromine-doped carbon quantum dots as shown in Fig. 5 . As expected, characteristic peaks for carbon (C 1s), oxygen (O 1s), and dopant bromine (Br 3d) were observed in Fig. 5-(a, b) at 533 eV, 285 eV, and 69 eV respectively. In addition, the high-resolution scan (refer to Fig. 5-(c-g)) reveals the sub-peaks of each element. For carbon, the peak was deconvoluted to three peaks with maxima positioned at 284.7 eV, 286.4 eV, and 288.5 eV for both undoped and doped carbon dots. These peaks can be attributed to C-C/C=C, CO, and CO respectively [49–51]. This indicates the presence of oxygen-related functional groups in the carbon dots necessary for the formation of oxidative species. The oxygen peak showed a maxima at 533.6 eV, which did not further resolve into multiple peaks [52]. The carbonyl bonds are responsible for the oxygen peak. The bromine peak is resolved into three distinct peaks at 67.91 eV, 68.96 eV, and 70.75 eV, which is attributed to Br orbitals Br d5/2, Br d3/2, Br− anion as shown in Fig. 5(g) [53–55]. This implies the successful doping of bromine in carbon dots. The CBr bonding can be further confirmed by a separate peak in the high-resolution plot for C 1s (Fig. 5–(e)) at 293 eV [56].

Fig. 6 (a) represents the UV absorption of the as-synthesized un-doped carbon dots. 50 μl of the supernatant was diluted to 2 ml with DI water for the characterization. The intrinsic carbon dots exhibit a broad UV absorption with two maxima at 244 nm and 294 nm and the absorption edge at 340 nm. Generally, the high energy peak absorption maxima are associated with π-π* transitions attributed to the carbogenic core of the carbon dots. Whereas, the low energy peaks are attributed to the n-π* transitions occurring at the surface states [57,58]. Doping is common strategy to which can introduce new intermediate energy levels. Doping halogen atoms in carbon dots modifies the electronic structure, optical properties, and luminescent properties of the carbon dots [59–61]. A blue shift (19 nm) was observed when the carbon dots are doped with halogens which brings the absorption band edge to 321 nm. Owing to the strong electronegative nature of halogen atoms, a blue shift in absorption spectra was observed. Electron-withdrawing groups have been observed to exhibit a hypsochromic shift in the UV–visible absorption [62,63]. Also, the size of the doped carbon dots is affected by the solubility of the dopant precursors (potassium salts of halides). The solubility of the potassium halide salts increases from KF to KI [64]. With increasing solubility, more and more halide ions become available during the nucleation and growth phases of the carbon dot formation [65,66]. This leads to the formation of smaller carbon dots, thus, the blue shift in the absorption peaks. Moreover, the characteristic double peaks have blue-shifted and combined to form a wider absorbance peak. Doping fluorine and iodine have increased absorption intensity significantly. A blue shift in the UV absorption also implies a wider bandgap which produces more reactive charge carriers and radical species during the photocatalytic degradation [67,68]. Tauc plot of undoped and halogen doped CD was used to calculate the band gap Eg

E g
where n represent type of electronic transition, n = 2 for direct allowed transitions, α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant and ν is the photon frequency. Fig. 6(b) represents the Tauc plot, confirming the increase in the optical bandgap of the carbon quantum dots. The band gap of the halogen-doped carbon quantum dots was found to increase with the addition of halogen dopants as indicated inset of Fig. 6(b). Doping can introduce surface and defect states. Coexistence of defect sites and isolated sp2 carbon clusters may effectively increase the band gap in the UV–visible region [58]. Carbon dots are also known to show photoluminescent (PL) emission which is mostly used for bio-imaging and sensing applications. PL emission characteristics of the samples were observed to show excitation-dependent emission. To evaluate the same, emission spectra of carbon dot solutions were recorded at multiple excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm as shown in Fig. S2. Samples were found to exhibit excitation-dependent emission characteristics.

The carbon dots were characterized for their zeta potential to understand the surface charge over carbon dots using Anton Paar Litesizer 500. The potential was measured at a temperature of 25 °C and the dispersion medium was taken as distilled water. In Fig. 7 , the zeta potential of all the carbon dot samples was observed to be negative which is a pre-requisite parameter for efficient photocatalytic degradation of MB. MB is known to be a cationic dye due to the presence of S+ heteroatom in the dye structure [69]. Thus, negative surface charges over as-prepared carbon dots aid the fast adsorption of dye followed by the efficient photocatalysis process. Electrostatic attraction between the positive dye molecules and the negative carbon dot facilitates efficient MB degradation. This can also be observed from the degree of adsorption of dye molecules during the photocatalytic degradation tests. Doping of iodine in carbon dots yields maximum negative surface charges owing to which a sharp reduction in intensity of absorption peak at time t = 0 min was observed as shown in Fig. 8 (e). This can be attributed to the higher electronegative nature of doped iodine forming a negative charge density.

Fig. 8(a) represents the UV–visible absorption spectra of the MB dye solution with un-doped carbon dot photo-catalyst at different time intervals. Carbon dot–dye solution in the quartz glass tube was irradiated by twelve 8 W Mercury UV light (254 nm) in a hexagonal photoreactor. Before performing photocatalysis, the carbon dot–dye solution was stirred for 30 min in a dark environment. During the photocatalytic process, aliquots of carbon dot–dye solution were extracted at different times (t = 0 to t = 90 min), and UV–visible spectra were recorded as shown in Fig. 8(a-e). Fig. 8(b-e) represents UV–visible absorption spectra of the MB dye solution with halogen doped carbon dot namely (b) fluorine, (c) chlorine, (d) bromine and (e) iodine respectively at different time intervals. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of intrinsic carbon dots was found to be 93 % under 90 min of UV exposure. However, F, Cl, Br and I doped samples were found to have enhanced efficiency of 98.6, 97.4, 99.2, and 98.3 % respectively. Out of all the halogen-doped samples, bromine doping yields the highest amount of degradation (99.23 %). The photocatalytic mechanism follows 4 major steps: (i) Adsorption of the dye molecule on the carbon quantum dot surface, (ii) change in the molecular structure of methylene blue (MB), (iii) absorption of UV radiation & separation of charge carriers, (iv) formation of radical species and simultaneous attack on the adsorbed methylene blue. The peak maxima in UV–visible spectra at 664 nm and 612 nm represent the N – S heteroatom ring in the methylene blue structure [70]. Owing to the change in the heteroatom ring structure of MB, a reduction in the intensity of the absorption peak was observed. This is attributed to the attack of radical species on the heteroatom ring under UV excitation and the absence of chromophoric intermediates formed during the degradation [71,72]. From Fig. 8(a)–(e), it was observed carbon dots doped with iodine (CD-I) could initially adsorb up to 50 % of the dye molecule followed by other carbon dots samples in order: CDI > UCD > CDBr > CDF > CDCl. This can be attributed to the high zeta potential of Iodine-doped CDs, negatively charged halogen ions and the surface functional groups (-COOH, -C-OH, -C=O) aids in the electrostatic attraction of cationic dye molecules for adsorption. During the adsorption, the positively charged S+ ion in the heteroatom ring of methylene blue is attracted to the negatively charged oxygen functional groups on the carbon quantum dot surface. The S+ forms a temporary bond with the oxygen atoms, becoming the electron donor and thus forming a sulfoxide bonding. The formation of sulfoxide bonding creates a chain reaction that changes the conjugate bond structure of the heteroatom aromatic ring [73,74]. Due to these changes, the nitrogen in the ring is open for chemical attack by the radicals formed by the photogenerated charge carriers as shown in Fig. 9 (b). The carbon dots were then subjected to a UV radiation source (λ = 254 nm). In the first 20 min of UV exposure, there is a significant difference in the reaction rate of intrinsic and halogen-doped carbon dot samples as shown in Fig. 8(f). The rate is observed to be inversely proportional to the amount of adsorbed dye on the surface. This is a result of the saturation of the carbon dot surface due to dye molecule adsorption which hinders the UV absorption by the carbon dots and subsequent radical generation [75,76]. Owing to electronegativity, high atomic weight (126.9 g/mol) and high negative zeta potential iodine-doped carbon dots quickly photodegrades MB within a few seconds of exposure to UV light, later showing a slower degradation of MB dye. The high molecular weight of dopants is known to enhance the photocatalytic degradation process [77,78]. 97 % of the initial concentration of MB dye was observed in 90 min of UV exposure. On the other hand, Br doped sample owing to its higher electronegativity than Iodine and moderate atomic weight shows a gradual and steady photodegradation process throughout the UV exposure and causes degradation of more than 99 % of initial concentration within 90 min of UV exposure unlike F, Cl and I doped samples which degrades up to 98 %, 97 %, 98 % of the dye respectively. Electronegativity difference between carbon and halogen atoms induces a local polarisation generating an internal electric field that prevents charge carrier recombination, thereby promoting charge separation which enhances the photocatalysis degradation process as compared to intrinsic carbon dots.

Doped and undoped carbon dots follow the photocatalytic reaction according to Langmuir – Hinshelwood Model, which implies the rate of reaction is linear and is dependent on the initial concentration of the MB. Langmuir-Hinshelwood model follows the kinetics as given by the Eq. (1):
(2)

C
C 0
= −
k app
t
where C and C0 are the concentrations of MB molecules before and after photocatalytic degradation, which are considered to be proportional to the UV–vis absorption intensities, respectively. kapp denotes the apparent rate of photocatalytic reaction, which is equal to the slope of the linear plot between ln(C/C0) and time of irradiation in the model, and t represents the time for which the degradation is carried out. Fig. S3 graphically represents the degradation kinetics of methylene blue on carbon dots. Table ST1 in the supplementary information, further elaborates on the degradation rate of MB by halogen-doped carbon dots. The degradation of MB dye molecules was also tested in a real water sample to check the efficacy of the bromine doped carbon dots and a similar degradation kinetics was observed as that of the DI water as shown in Fig. S6.

Here we consider three radicals to mainly contribute to the degradation of dye molecules i.e. h+ (positively charged holes), •OH (hydroxyl radical), •O2-(superoxide anion radical). The contributions of each radical were experimentally verified using radical scavengers in photocatalytic degradation experiments and shown in Fig. S4. Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), para-benzoquinone (p-BZQ), and ethylene-diamine tetra-acetic acid (EDTA) were used as scavengers for reactive hydroxyl radicals (OH•), anionic superoxide radicals (•O2−), and positive holes (h+) respectively. It was observed that for UCD, CD-Cl, and CD-I, hydroxyl radicals were the major contributors to photocatalytic degradation. For CD-Br and CD-F superoxide radicals and photogenerated holes were responsible for the degradation of methylene blue molecules respectively. The generation of each radical follows a separate pathway of methylene blue degradation. After the adsorption of the dye molecule and opening up the N-S heteroatom ring, the nitrogen outside the aromatic rings undergoes hydrogenation and the carbon in the aromatic ring undergoes demethylation to form many aromatic intermediates and finally gets demineralized into CO2, NO3 −, SO4 2−, H2O, etc. [79,80]. The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared halogen-doped carbon dots was compared with the existing literature on intrinsic and metal-doped carbon dots as shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that as-prepared halogen carbon is efficient enough to cause 99 % degradation within 90 min.

The mechanistic pathways for the degradation of methylene blue and the reaction intermediates were identified and confirmed using a high-performance liquid chromatograph and mass spectrometer (HPLC-MS). The HPLC and MS plots have been shown in the supplementary information (Fig. S6a-b). The MB molecules and their degradation intermediates were identified from the mass-to-charge (m/z) ratio detected by the mass spectrometer, and the degradation pathway is as shown in Fig. 10 . The charge-to-mass ratio for methylene blue is assigned to a value of 284 [87]. The intensity of the peak at m/z = 284 was observed to decrease at time = 0 mins, and a subsequent peak at m/z = 301 (3,7-bis(dimethylamino)-10H-phenothiazine 5-oxide) was observed among other peaks. This is attributed to the formation of sulphoxide bonds in the methylene blue molecules [73,74]. Apart from the formation of sulphoxide bonds, the peaks at m/z = 227 and m/z = 318 explain the pathways where the degradation occurs through demethylation of MB molecules, where the -CH3 attached to the heteroatomic nitrogen is replaced by a hydrogen atom. The electrophilic attack of •OH on the sulphoxide bond further destabilizes and cleaves the heteroatomic aromatic ring in the MB molecule, producing the intermediates with the m/z = 167 and 230 [88,89]. Further degradation of the molecules occurs by the hydroxylation, cleavage of the phenolic and sulphonyl benzene fractions, which yields the intermediates with m/z = 158, 143, 142, 109, and finally converted into CO2, SO2, and H2O.

Carbon dots are well known for their optical fluorescence due to their photoluminescence emission tunable surface states and particle size [90,91]. This property has been applied in various wastewater and biological sensing applications. In this study, the authors have tried to utilize the fluorescence property of carbon dots to sense metal ions in aqueous media. To investigate the selectivity of the metal ions sensing, the fluorescent intensity of undoped carbon quantum dots was tested in aqueous solutions of metal ions which include Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, Hg2+, Pb2+ and Co2+ ions. In current studies, UCD (undoped carbon dots) were used to sense Cu2+ ions with a concentration range 0–1000 μM in DI water. Doped carbon dots were found to be less effective and selective towards Cu2+ ions. Thus, UCDs were used for sensing application. Fig. 11 (a) represents the relative fluorescent intensities of the carbon dots solutions in the presence of different metal ions. A minor reduction in emission intensity was observed (10–15 %) upon adding Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, Co2+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ metal ions. To evaluate the selectivity of the carbon dots towards Cu2+ ions, interference studies were also carried out as shown in Fig. S7. Ions such as Co2+, Hg2+, Pb2+ hardly show any quenching effect on carbon dots, however, as soon as Cu2+ ions were introduced, a drastic reduction in the emission intensity can be seen. Slight interference was observed in case of Cr3+ ions. On the other hand, Fe2+ and Fe3+ shows slightly higher influence on the carbon dot solution. In addition to the static quenching mechanism, electrostatic interaction between different metal ions and carbon dots also plays an important role in fluorescent sensing applications. The change in UV absorption in the presence of Fe(NO3)3 and Cr(NO3)3 suggests a formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex similar to that in the presence of Cu2+ ions. This can be explained by the hard-soft-acid -base theory (HSAB) where the functional groups on the carbon dots surface act as a base (electron-donating species) and metal ions act as acids (electron-accepting species) [92,93]. Here, the electronegativity, the size, and the magnitude of positive charge of the metal ion determine the strength of acidity, whereas the basicity of the carbon dots depends upon the heteroatomic element present in the functional group or the type of functional groups. The softer acids like Cu2+ can be detected by softer bases like oxygen-containing functional groups. The metals with comparable electronegativity values can form stable complexes with softer bases [94]. However, iron and chromium ions have a higher electronegativity, thus, higher acidity, which require a stronger base like N-containing functional groups on the surface of carbon dots [95,96]. Thus, as prepared carbon dots were found to be selective to Cu2+ ions. Cu (II) is one of the heavy metals released into water bodies due to improper disposal of fertilizers and pesticides. Though Cu2+ is an essential micronutrient, overexposure to excess concentration of Cu2+ is linked with serious health issues such as kidney failure, diabetes, liver damage etc. [97–100] Thus, it is important to sense and regulate the concentration of Cu(II) ions in aqueous bodies.

For the quantification of the quenching effects of copper ions, fluorescence spectroscopy was conducted for different concentrations of Cu2+ ions in the solution (0–1000 μM). The reduction of fluorescent intensity was observed with increasing concentration of copper ions which is illustrated in Fig. 11(c). The relationship between the relative fluorescent intensities (I0/I) and concentration of metal ions in the test solutions was observed to be linear with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.9984 and follows the Stern-Volmer's equation given by Eq. (2):
(3)
I 0
I
= 1 +
K sv
Q
where I and I0 are the fluorescent intensities in the presence and absence of copper ions whereas [Q] represents the concentration of Cu ions that was varied from 200 to 1000 μM (Fig. 11(d)). KSV denotes the Stern-Volmer constant for fluorescent quenching. The Stern-Volmer constant was calculated to be 6.8097× 10−4 M−1. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 131 μM by Eq. (3):
(4)
LOD = 3 σ / S
where σ denotes the standard deviation of the blank solution (n = 6), whereas S denotes the slope of the linear plot. The linearity of reduction in the fluorescent intensity with the concentration of metal ions even at higher concentrations of metal ions indicates the role of dynamic quenching mechanisms and a positive deviation in the plot suggests the quenching to be static in nature [101]. Fig. 11(b) illustrates the formation of the ground state complex between the positively charged metallic cations and negatively charged functional groups on the carbon dot surface [102–104]. The energy transfer between the functional groups and metal ions reduces the fluorescence intensity as per the static quenching process. The ground state complex between Cu2+ ions and carbon dots being non-fluorescent, reduces the photoluminescence emission intensity of carbon dots gradually are the concentration of Cu2+ ions increases. To further confirm the mechanism, time-resolved fluorescence studies and UV absorption studies were conducted for carbon dots in the presence and absence of the metal ions, as shown in Fig. S5(a and b). The lifetimes of the carbon dots do not show any significant changes upon introduction of Cu2+ ions whereas, the UV–Vis absorption of carbon dots shows a shift in the peak positioned at 224 nm of the carbon dots in the presence of Cu2+ ions. The shift of UV–visible absorption implies an irreversible change in the surface state of the carbon dots, which can be a result of Cu2+ ions forming a complex with the oxygen functionalities [105–107]. Such results can be observed in the mechanism of statically quenched fluorophores, where the formation of metal ion‑carbon dot complexes reduces the distance between the carbon dots [108]. This facilitates non-radiative energy transfer, which is observed as a reduction in fluorescent intensity.

The current study dives deep into the capabilities of intrinsic and halogen-doped carbon dots in the field of dye degradation and selective heavy metal sensing of Cu (II) ions. Ascorbic acid-derived undoped and halogen–doped carbon dots were synthesized with an average size of 9.5 and 4.5 ± 1.5 nm, respectively. Halogen doping was also confirmed via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic studies. Doped carbon dots exhibited a blue shift in their absorption characteristics, which enabled them to absorb the UV radiation from the photoreactor to a greater extent, providing a higher degradation efficiency compared to the undoped carbon dots. Degradation mechanism was studied via LCMS studies. The bromine-doped sample yields the highest photocatalytic efficiency out of all the other samples (99.24 %). Undoped carbon dots were also found to exhibit static fluorescent quenching in the presence of Cu2+ ion, owing to which these halogen carbon dots can also be utilized as a fluorescent sensing probe. Undoped carbon dots were selectively sensitive towards Cu2+ ions compared to Fe3+, Fe2+, Cr2+, Sn4+, and Co2+ ions.
Akshay M. Achari: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. G. Swati: Writing – review & editing, Supervision.
